data.title: Dollar dips ahead of key central bank decisions, Iran tensions linger: Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. dollar edged lower on Monday, with investors cautious amid uncertainty over U.S.-Iran developments and ahead of key central bank meetings this week.

The dollar index (DXY), which measures the greenback against a basket of major currencies, was last down 0.06% to $98.47 but remains up about 0.25% for the year to date.

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers:

Over the past week, the index advanced 0.29%, with the greenback’s trajectory shaped by shifting expectations around the U.S.-Iran conflict and evolving central bank outlooks.

Early in the period, the greenback found support as tensions in the Middle East escalated, with Iran tightening control over the Strait of Hormuz and seizing vessels, raising concerns over prolonged disruptions to global energy supplies and prompting safe-haven demand.

This strength was reinforced by a growing consensus that major central banks would adopt a wait-and-see approach, as policymakers assessed the inflationary implications of higher oil prices. Expectations that the Federal Reserve and its global peers would hold rates steady, alongside a resilient U.S. economic backdrop, further underpinned the dollar.

However, sentiment remained fluid, and the dollar came under pressure at times as diplomatic efforts to revive talks resurfaced, with reports of a new Iranian proposal and continued mediation keeping markets cautious.

Later in the week, the dollar’s gains moderated as the market grappled with competing forces, persistent geopolitical risks supporting safe-haven flows, while shifting expectations around U.S. monetary policy, including the potential transition in Federal Reserve leadership, introduced a more dovish undertone.

By the end of the period, the dollar edged lower as traders positioned cautiously ahead of a heavy slate of central bank meetings, including what is expected to be Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s final policy decision, with markets bracing for guidance on the economic fallout from the ongoing conflict.

During the period, global markets were influenced by developments in the U.S.-Iran conflict, fluctuations in oil prices, evolving inflation expectations, and anticipation of key central bank policy signals.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rising nine basis points to 4.35%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose seven basis points to 3.81%.

Currency Highlights:

Major currency movements (April 20 to April 26)

Euro (EUR:USD)

-0.39%

Pound Sterling (GBP:USD)

+0.20%

Japanese Yen (JPY:USD)

-0.16%

Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD)

-0.20%

Swiss Franc (CHF:USD)

-0.73%

Australian Dollar (AUD:USD)

+0.10%

Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD)

-0.13%

The euro (EUR:USD) was last down 0.01%, with the currency trading in a narrow range as European markets remained mixed amid persistent geopolitical tensions and uncertainty around U.S.-Iran developments.

Sentiment was further weighed by weak economic data, with Euro Area composite PMI slipping into contraction territory, while investors stayed cautious ahead of key central bank decisions.

The pound sterling (GBP:USD) was last up 0.01%, holding steady as investors weighed mixed domestic signals alongside broader geopolitical uncertainty.

U.K. economic data showed inflation rising and unemployment easing, while sentiment remained cautious amid global developments and ahead of policy direction.

The Swiss franc (CHF:USD) was last down 0.08%, extending its weekly decline as the currency remained under pressure amid broader market uncertainty.

data.title: Rithm Capital rebrands Paramount Group as Elecor Properties
data.description:
Rithm Capital (RITM) renamed Paramount Group, its commercial real estate platform, as Elecor Partners, the company said Monday. Furthermore, with key joint venture partners, the company plans for ~$250M in capital improvements across Elecor's portfolio.

Paramount Group was acquired by Rithm in December, enhancing Rithm's asset management platform and commercial real estate footprint.

"The new identity reflects a strategic transformation from a high-quality, Class A office portfolio into an operating platform built for the next evolution of the workplace," the company said.

Elecor's leadership team remains in place, with Peter Brindley continuing as its executive vice president, head of real estate, overseeing day-to-day operations.

Rithm Capital (RITM) stock rose 1.1% in after-hours trading

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate Equities trades lower as potential tenant wind-downs hit Q1 earnings
data.description:
Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) was trading lower after the REIT reported lower first-quarter earnings on the back of potential tenant wind-downs.

Shares were -2.00% during Monday after-market trading at $44.65.

The health care REIT reported Q1 adjusted FFO per share of $1.73, in line with the consensus, but below the year-ago level of $2.30 and $2.16 in the prior quarter.

Revenue of $671.02M stood below the year-ago $762.04M and $758.16M recorded in the prior quarter. The metric stood below the consensus revenue estimate of $695.55M. 

Income from rentals fell to $653.01M from $728.87M in the previous quarter and $743.18M a year ago.

Operating occupancy as of March 31 stood at 87.7%.

The Pasadena, California-based company narrowed its 2026 adjusted FFO per share guidance range to $6.30-$6.50 from $6.25-$6.55. The midpoint stands at $6.40, which compares with a consensus of $6.40.

The guidance revision includes a $25M-$30M reduction in funds from operations for potential tenant wind-downs, of which ~$6M was recognized in 1Q26.

Furthermore, the company recognized development fees and other related revenues of ~$2.5M during Q1, most of which are expected to cease by the end of 2026.

Alexandria Real Estate Equities is set to introduce its 2027 guidance at the Investor Day on December 2.

Earnings conference is scheduled for Tuesday, April 28, at 2:00 PM ET.

data.title: LendingClub surges after Q1 earnings beat on hefty loan origination growth, accounting change
data.description:
LendingClub (LC) stock soared 14% in Monday after-hours trading after the online lender's Q1 earnings and revenue handily topped Wall Street expectations, as loan originations grew 31% Y/Y and an accounting change lowered its provision for credit losses. Furthermore, the company issued solid Q2 guidance and reaffirmed its full-year outlook.

The online lending platform  expects Q2 GAAP EPS of $0.40-$0.45 (midpoint to $0.43), vs. the average analyst estimate of $0.41, and loan originations of $3.0B-$3.1B, vs. the Visible Alpha consensus of $3.0B.

It reaffirmed its full-year guidance for EPS of $1.65-$1.80 (midpoint $1.73) vs. the $1.71 consensus and loan originations of $11.6B-$12.6B vs. the Visible Alpha estimate of $12.1B.

Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.44, beating the average analyst estimate of $0.36, rose/fell from $0.35 in Q4 2025 and $0.10 in last year’s Q1.

Q1 total net revenue of $252.3M, topping the $249.1M consensus, decreased from $266.5M in the previous quarter and increased from $217.7M in the year-ago period.

"We're starting 2026 with exceptional momentum, delivering 31% year-over-year growth in originations while achieving record pre-tax earnings of $67 million and ROTCE of 14.5%," said CEO Scott Sanborn.

During the quarter, LendingClub (LC) announced its Happen Bank will launch in summer 2026, reflecting  expanded banking capabilities. After the end of Q1, the company started underwriting and originating home improvement loans.

Starting in Q1, the company adopted fair option accounting for all new originations of loans held for investment. This change aligns the accounting treatment for loans held for investment and held for sale, creating a consistent framework across the business and removing the front-loaded current expected credit loss ((CECL)) reserve impact that corresponds to balance sheet growth. The company expects this transition will, over time, result in a higher return on invested capital.

Total loan originations climbed to $2.67B from $2.64B in Q4 and $2.03B in Q1 2025. The current quarter exceeded the Visible Alpha estimate of $2.63B.

Provision for credit losses fell to $390K, reflecting the change to FVO accounting, from $47.2M in the previous quarter and $58.1M in the year-ago period.

Q1  income before income tax expense grew to $67.3M from $50.0M in Q4 and $11.7M a year ago.

Return on tangible common equity of 14.5% for Q1 increased from 11.9% in Q4 and 3.7% a year ago.

Conference call at 5:00 PM ET.

Earlier, LendingClub GAAP EPS of $0.44 beats by $0.08, revenue of $252.25M beats by $3.17M

data.title: Paramount Skydance flags 49.5% foreign ownership to fund Warner Bros. acquisition in FCC filing
data.description:
Following Paramount Skydance’s (PSKY) acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), the combined company will be nearly half owned by non-U.S. investors, an issue downplayed by the media conglomerate and flagged by rival Netflix (NFLX) when the two were engaged in a bidding war.

According to a filing with the Federal Communications Commission, Paramount (PSKY) said that the acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) will be partially financed by three Middle East investment funds that will own a combined 24% of the new company.

But despite attempts to structure the deal to avoid oversight by the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States ((CFIUS)), Paramount acknowledges that foreign entities will own more than the 25% threshold but that these investments “serve the public interest,” as it would allow Paramount “greater access to capital, including from foreign sources,” to compete more effectively in the TV and streaming marketplace.

This includes the broadcast of local and national news, “enhancing the availability of reliable, high-quality, non-paywalled local and national news.” By addressing the indirect ownership of U.S. news stations, Paramount assures regulators that the Ellison family will retain a majority of the voting interest and control of the parent company. Following the acquisition, Paramount Skydance (PSKY) will own CNN as well as its current ownership of CBS News and affiliated stations across the country.

The filing also notes that foreign investors will not have voting rights in the new company.

data.title: Alliance Resource Partners outlines ~5% increase to 2026 oil and gas volume guidance while coal sales move to over 95% committed
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Alliance delivered a solid first quarter with adjusted EBITDA exceeding our internal target due to record BOE volumes and higher commodity prices and increased oil & gas royalties revenues." (Chairman, President & CEO Joseph Craft)
  • "Overall, the quarterly results came in higher than expected due to record BOE volumes and higher commodity prices that increased oil and gas royalties revenues." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "Temporary weather-related disruptions caused approximately 200,000 tons of scheduled shipments to be delayed." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "Net income in the 2026 quarter reflected lower coal sales revenue, higher depreciation, an $11.6 million decrease in the fair value of our digital assets and a $37.8 million non-cash asset impairment charge at our Mettiki mine." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "We continue to evaluate the appropriate path forward for Mettiki, though meaningful uncertainty remains and greater clarity is not expected until later this year." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "In the interim, our priority at Mettiki is to reduce costs while preserving the flexibility and optionality needed to align future operations with customer demand." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "As noted during prior calls, pricing is normalizing as higher-priced legacy coal contracts entered into during the 2022 energy crisis continue to roll off and are being replaced at coal pricing levels consistent with our current guidance ranges." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "In late March, we also successfully completed the final phase of our multiyear River View to Henderson County miner unit transition, bringing the Henderson County mine up to its planned full production capacity of 6 super sections." (Chairman, President & CEO Joseph Craft)
  • "Our oil & gas business delivered another record quarter, driven by growth in volumes from increased drilling and completion activity by our operating partners and contributions from recent acquisitions." (Chairman, President & CEO Joseph Craft)

Outlook

  • Analysts’ estimates were provided, but the JSON uses numeric fiscal quarters (not Q1–Q4), so the comparison to estimates was skipped.
  • "We are maintaining our overall guidance ranges for coal sales volumes, coal sales price and segment adjusted EBITDA expense per ton." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "We will complete planned longwall move activity for the year during the upcoming quarter and with no additional longwall moves anticipated until the first quarter of 2027, we expect better operational visibility in the second half of 2026." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "We layered on $2.6 million net contracted tons for delivery in 2026 and 2027." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "Our 2026 expected coal sales volumes are now more than 95% committed and priced at the midpoint of our guidance ranges." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "The most notable changes to our guidance are in the Oil & Gas Royalty segment, where year-to-date volumes have exceeded our initial expectations." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "Based on that outperformance, we are increasing our 2026 volume guidance by approximately 5% on a BOE basis." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "If current strip pricing is realized, we expect realized BOE prices to be higher than last year, supporting stronger segment adjusted EBITDA." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)

Financial Results

  • "For the first quarter of 2026... adjusted EBITDA was $155 million." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "Net income attributable to ARLP in the 2026 quarter was $9.1 million or $0.07 per unit." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "In the 2026 quarter, total revenues were $516 million." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "Our average coal sales price per ton for the 2026 quarter was $56.40." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "Total coal production in the 2026 quarter was 8 million tons" and "Coal sales volumes were 7.9 million tons." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "Total royalty revenues were $61.2 million" including "Oil & Gas Royalty revenues... $41.3 million" and "record BOE volumes of 1 million." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "As of March 31, 2026, total debt and finance leases were outstanding in the amount of $507.7 million" and "our total and net leverage ratios were 0.73x and 0.69x debt to trailing 12 months adjusted EBITDA." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "We also held 618 Bitcoin valued at $42.2 million at quarter end based on $68,233 per coin." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)
  • "We reported distributable cash flow of $77.8 million" and "our distribution coverage ratio for the quarter was 1x." (Senior VP & CFO Cary Marshall)

Q&A

  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company: "should we assume now that, that valve has closed?" and "what API2 price range ARLP needs to incentivize sales"; Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "primarily, that number is around 120" and "there still could be possibilities of increased export opportunities" but "our current posture though, is to really focus on opportunities that are available to us in the domestic market."
  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company: summer demand and downside risk; Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "we're seeing our customers going out and looking to add to their position" and "most forecasts are projecting that the summer would be warmer than normal."
  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company: PJM reliability and data centers; Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "we must keep every coal plant, every natural gas plant, all generation online" and "we're hearing more and more that are announcing staying open to 2034 as a minimum."
  • Matthew Key, Texas Capital Securities: Appalachia cost cadence; Senior VP & CFO Marshall: "all the longwall moves in Appalachia are completed" and "it could be somewhere in that neighborhood 15%, 20% quarter-over-quarter" cost reduction as volumes rise.
  • Matthew Key, Texas Capital Securities: balancing Gavin and minerals; Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "if there are those owners of coal plants that are interested in divesting those, we are definitely interested in participating in that" and "there is the potential to invest more in the oil & gas at the right underwriting standards."
  • Mark La Reichman, NOBLE Capital Markets: funding growth and hurdle rates; Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "coal... payback period in a shorter time period" and "the return is anywhere from 15% to 20-something percent" on minerals, depending on risk profile.
  • Mark La Reichman, NOBLE Capital Markets: Bitcoin strategy; Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "we do believe that the upside on pricing is significant enough that we should hold on to what we have" and "we're of the view that it's got more upside than downside."
  • Michael Mathison, Sidoti: Appalachia pricing vs. guidance; Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "those sales contracts are rolling off" and "a larger percent of Tunnel Ridge production... means lower revenue compared to what we had at Mettiki."
  • Michael Mathison, Sidoti: CapEx run rate and other income; Senior VP & CFO Marshall: "we did purchase about $15.5 million of coal reserves" within "a little bit over $95 million" CapEx, and other income included "a favorable actuarial adjustment" plus "a favorable adjustment to the valuation of our holdings of Infinitum."
  • Michael Mathison, Sidoti: outside coal purchases; Senior VP & CFO Marshall: "Our expectation is no additional outside coal purchases." Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "That tied directly to our Mettiki operation."
  • Edward Easton, Easton Group Companies: buybacks or distribution increase; Chairman, President & CEO Craft: "we need to get our distribution coverage ratio more in line with an expectation of 1.2x to 1.4x... before we would consider either of those."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on export market durability, demand sensitivity to summer weather, cost normalization, CapEx variance, Bitcoin volatility, and capital return timing (e.g., "When it's going down, it could be a bit of a distraction." (Mark La Reichman, NOBLE Capital Markets)).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing recovery of disruptions and improved visibility (e.g., "we expect the first quarter shipment disruptions... to be recovered" (Chairman, President & CEO Craft) and "meaningful uncertainty remains" at Mettiki (Senior VP & CFO Marshall)).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management kept a similar confidence level on contracting and long-term demand, while the current call carried more explicit near-term caution around Mettiki path forward and distribution coverage at "1x" in Q1 (Senior VP & CFO Marshall).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance framing shifted from introducing 2026 ranges in Q4 to "maintaining" coal ranges in Q1 while "increasing" Oil & Gas Royalty volume guidance by "approximately 5%" (Senior VP & CFO Marshall).
  • Contracting moved from "more than 93%" committed for 2026 in Q4 to "more than 95%" committed in Q1, alongside added commitments including "2 million tons" tied to a brief export window (Chairman, President & CEO Craft).
  • The Mettiki narrative evolved from Q4 warnings about customer outages and the likelihood of impairment review to Q1 reporting an actual "$37.8 million" impairment and continued uncertainty on future operations (Senior VP & CFO Marshall).
  • Analysts’ focus broadened from Q4 themes (cadence, pricing into 2027, and equity-method income) toward Q1 scrutiny of Bitcoin strategy, capital allocation between minerals and power, and whether export pricing remains viable.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Temporary weather-related disruptions" delayed shipments and created near-term volume timing risk, though management said shipments should be recovered (Senior VP & CFO Marshall).
  • "Meaningful uncertainty" at Mettiki remained a central operational and earnings risk, with management emphasizing cost reduction and optionality while expecting clarity "until later this year" (Senior VP & CFO Marshall).
  • Coal pricing risk continued as legacy contracts roll off: "pricing is normalizing" as older contracts are replaced (Senior VP & CFO Marshall).
  • Unhedged royalties exposure was positioned as both upside and risk: "With the portfolio unhedged, changes in commodity prices directly impact our realized pricing" (Chairman, President & CEO Craft).

Final Takeaway

ARLP management described Q1 as a quarter where record oil and gas royalties volumes and pricing lifted results above internal targets, while coal fundamentals were steady but affected by weather-delayed shipments and planned longwall downtime. Management maintained 2026 coal guidance, raised Oil & Gas Royalty volume guidance by about 5% on a BOE basis, and said 2026 coal volumes are now more than 95% committed and priced at the midpoint of guidance ranges. The call repeatedly returned to Mettiki uncertainty—now including a reported non-cash impairment—alongside a stated priority to manage costs and preserve flexibility, while investor questions centered on export optionality, capital allocation between minerals and coal-fired power investments, the ongoing rationale for Bitcoin exposure, and the path back to a higher distribution coverage ratio before buybacks or distribution increases are considered.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
